question: What is file descriptor 0 (FD 0) in a Linux process?
answers: Standard Input (stdin)
Standard Output (stdout)
Standard Error (stderr)
Standard Log (syslog)
correct: Standard Input (stdin)

question: What is file descriptor 1 (FD 1) in a Linux process?
answers: Standard Input (stdin)
Standard Output (stdout)
Standard Error (stderr)
Standard Log (syslog)
correct: Standard Output (stdout)

question: What is file descriptor 2 (FD 2) in a Linux process?
answers: Standard Input (stdin)
Standard Output (stdout)
Standard Error (stderr)
Standard Debug (debug)
correct: Standard Error (stderr)

question: On x86-64 Linux, which register holds the syscall number?
answers: rdi
rbx
rax
rcx
correct: rax

question: On x86-64 Linux, which register holds the first syscall argument?
answers: rsi
rdi
rbx
rcx
correct: rdi

question: On x86-64 Linux, which register holds the second syscall argument?
answers: rdi
rdx
rsi
r8
correct: rsi

question: On x86-64 Linux, which register holds the third syscall argument?
answers: rsi
rdx
r8
rcx
correct: rdx

question: Which instruction is used to invoke a syscall on x86-64 Linux (not legacy x86)?
answers: int 0x80
sysenter
syscall
int 0x3
correct: syscall

question: Which instruction is the traditional syscall interface on x86 (32-bit) Linux?
answers: syscall
sysenter
int 0x80
int 0x3
correct: int 0x80

question: On x86-64 Linux, what is the syscall number for read()?
On x86-64 Linux, what is the syscall number for write()?
On x86-64 Linux, what is the syscall number for open()?
On x86 (32-bit) Linux, what is the syscall number for write()?
On x86 (32-bit) Linux, what is the syscall number for execve()?
On x86-64 Linux, what is the syscall number for execve()?
On x86-64 Linux, what is the syscall number for exit()?
What does read() return when it reaches end-of-file (EOF)?
answers: -1 (error occurred)
1 (success flag)
0 (EOF reached)
EOF (-2 magic value)
correct: 0 (EOF reached)

question: What value does fork() return inside the child process?
answers: The parent's PID

question: What value does fork() return inside the parent process on success?
answers: The child's PID
correct: The child's PID

question: In which direction does the stack grow on x86/x86-64 Linux?
answers: Upward (toward higher addresses)
Downward (toward lower addresses)
Bidirectional depending on frame size
Fixed size, does not grow dynamically
correct: Downward (toward lower addresses)

question: In which direction does the heap grow on Linux?
answers: Downward (toward lower addresses)
Fixed size, does not grow dynamically
Upward (toward higher addresses)
Bidirectional from a central midpoint
correct: Upward (toward higher addresses)

question: What does the .bss section of an ELF binary contain?
answers: Executable machine code instructions
Initialized global and static variables
Uninitialized global and static variables
Read-only string literals and constants
correct: Uninitialized global and static variables

question: What does the .data section of an ELF binary contain?
answers: Executable machine code instructions
Initialized global and static variables
Uninitialized global and static variables
Read-only string literals and constants
correct: Initialized global and static variables

question: What does the .text section of an ELF binary contain?
answers: Executable machine code instructions
Initialized global and static variables
Uninitialized global and static variables
Read-only string literals and constants
correct: Executable machine code instructions

question: What does the .rodata section of an ELF binary contain?
answers: Executable machine code instructions
Initialized global and static variables
Uninitialized global and static variables
Read-only string literals and constants
correct: Read-only string literals and constants

question: What are the first 4 bytes (magic number) of a valid ELF binary?
answers: \x00ELF
\x7fELF
MZ\x90\x00
\x7fBIN
correct: \x7fELF

question: What is the purpose of the PLT (Procedure Linkage Table) in an ELF binary?
answers: Stores resolved addresses of global variables for ASLR relocation
Provides stubs that resolve external library function addresses at call time
Maps virtual addresses to physical pages in the kernel page table
Stores exported function symbols for use by the dynamic linker
correct: Provides stubs that resolve external library function addresses at call time

question: What is the purpose of the GOT (Global Offset Table) in an ELF binary?
answers: Stores addresses of global variables and resolved library functions
Provides stubs that resolve external function addresses at call time
Maps virtual addresses to physical memory pages in kernel space
Contains the binary's exported symbol table for the dynamic linker
correct: Stores addresses of global variables and resolved library functions

question: Which Linux security mitigation makes the GOT read-only after relocations are resolved?
answers: ASLR
NX/DEP
RELRO
PIE
correct: RELRO

question: What does PIE (Position Independent Executable) do to an ELF binary?
answers: Marks the stack and heap as non-executable memory pages
Places a canary value before the saved return address on the stack
Randomises the base load address of the binary itself at runtime
Makes the GOT read-only after all relocations are completed
correct: Randomises the base load address of the binary itself at runtime

question: What does the NX (No-Execute) bit / DEP protection prevent?
answers: ASLR from being disabled by a user-space process at runtime
Randomisation of the stack and heap layout in virtual memory
Execution of code placed on pages marked non-executable (stack, heap)
Overwriting the saved return address on the stack with a gadget
correct: Execution of code placed on pages marked non-executable (stack, heap)

question: What is a stack canary used for in compiled Linux binaries?
answers: Randomising the binary's base address at load time
Detecting stack buffer overflows before the saved return address is used
Marking stack and heap pages as non-executable in the page table
Restricting which system calls a process is allowed to execute
correct: Detecting stack buffer overflows before the saved return address is used

question: What value in /proc/sys/kernel/randomize_va_space enables full ASLR?
What does /proc/self/maps show for the current process?
answers: The list of all open file descriptors and their target paths
The mapped virtual memory regions with permissions and offsets
The full history of system calls made since process start
The process ID, parent PID, and current scheduling priority
correct: The mapped virtual memory regions with permissions and offsets

question: What delimiter does /proc/self/cmdline use to separate arguments?
answers: Space characters (0x20)
Newline characters (0x0a)
Null bytes (0x00)
Comma characters (0x2c)
correct: Null bytes (0x00)

question: What does /proc/self/exe symlink point to?
answers: The current working directory of the running process
The executable binary file that launched the current process
The main shared library (libc) loaded by the process
The interpreter path from the ELF PT_INTERP segment
correct: The executable binary file that launched the current process

question: What is the Linux signal number for SIGSEGV (segmentation fault)?
What is the Linux signal number for SIGKILL?
What is the Linux signal number for SIGINT (generated by Ctrl+C)?
What is the Linux signal number for SIGTERM (default signal sent by kill)?
Which signal cannot be caught, blocked, or ignored by a user-space process?
answers: SIGTERM (15)
SIGINT (2)
SIGKILL (9)
SIGHUP (1)
correct: SIGKILL (9)

question: What is the octal value of the SUID permission bit on Linux?
What is the octal value of the SGID permission bit on Linux?
What is the octal value of the sticky bit on Linux?
What does getuid() return when running a SUID-root binary as a normal user?
answers: 0 — the effective UID set by the SUID owner of the file
The real UID of the user who executed the binary
-1 to signal that elevated privileges are currently active
The GID of the file owner instead of the numeric UID
correct: The real UID of the user who executed the binary

question: What does geteuid() return when running a SUID-root binary as a normal user?
answers: The real UID of the user who launched the process
0 — the effective UID from the SUID bit's file owner
-1 indicating a privilege escalation event has occurred
The GID of the file owner rather than the effective UID
correct: 0 — the effective UID from the SUID bit's file owner

question: What is the format of each line in /etc/passwd?
answers: username:password_hash:uid:gid:gecos:home:shell
username:x:uid:gid:gecos:home:shell
uid:gid:username:password_hash:home:shell
username:uid:gid:home:shell:password_hash
correct: username:x:uid:gid:gecos:home:shell

question: What does the 'x' in /etc/passwd's password field indicate?
answers: The account is locked and cannot be used to log in
The password hash is stored in /etc/shadow instead
The user account has no password set at all
The account requires two-factor authentication to use
correct: The password hash is stored in /etc/shadow instead

question: What does LD_PRELOAD allow on Linux?
answers: Disabling ASLR for a specific process on next execution
Loading a custom shared library before all others to intercept calls
Injecting shellcode directly into a running process via /proc
Overriding the kernel's syscall dispatch table for one process
correct: Loading a custom shared library before all others to intercept calls

question: Why is system() dangerous to use inside a SUID binary?
answers: system() calls execve() directly, which drops all SUID privileges
system() is implemented in assembly and ignores the NX protection
An attacker can manipulate the PATH variable to run an arbitrary binary
system() cannot handle null bytes and silently truncates the command
correct: An attacker can manipulate the PATH variable to run an arbitrary binary

question: What does reading from /dev/null always return?
answers: An infinite stream of null (0x00) bytes on every read call
Immediately EOF — read() returns 0 bytes read
An infinite stream of cryptographically random bytes
Blocks indefinitely waiting for data to become available
correct: Immediately EOF — read() returns 0 bytes read

question: What does reading from /dev/zero always return?
answers: Immediately EOF — read() returns 0 bytes read
An infinite stream of cryptographically random bytes
An infinite stream of null (0x00) bytes
Blocks until keyboard input is received on the terminal
correct: An infinite stream of null (0x00) bytes

question: What is /dev/urandom used for?
answers: Generating an infinite stream of null bytes for memory zeroing
Providing a non-blocking source of cryptographically random bytes
Discarding all data written to it with no side effects
Generating deterministic pseudorandom output for reproducible tests
correct: Providing a non-blocking source of cryptographically random bytes

question: What sequence does rand() produce if srand() is never called (as in pwnable.kr's random)?
answers: Truly random values sourced from /dev/urandom at startup
All zeroes since the seed defaults to zero
A deterministic sequence seeded with 1 — identical every run
An error (-1) on the first call because no seed was provided
correct: A deterministic sequence seeded with 1 — identical every run

question: In the pwnable.kr 'fd' challenge, what happens when read() is called with fd = 0?
answers: read() fails with EBADF because 0 is not a valid open descriptor
read() reads from /dev/null, returning 0 bytes immediately
read() reads input from stdin (terminal or piped data)
read() reads from /dev/zero, returning infinite null bytes
correct: read() reads input from stdin (terminal or piped data)

question: What does dup2(oldfd, newfd) do?
answers: Creates a hard link between two already-open file descriptors
Duplicates oldfd so that newfd now refers to the same underlying file
Swaps the positions of two file descriptors atomically in one call
Creates a pipe with oldfd as write end and newfd as read end
correct: Duplicates oldfd so that newfd now refers to the same underlying file

question: What does the pipe() syscall return in its pipefd array?
answers: A single fd for bidirectional read/write communication
pipefd[0] for reading and pipefd[1] for writing
pipefd[0] for writing and pipefd[1] for reading
A socketpair for full-duplex inter-process communication
correct: pipefd[0] for reading and pipefd[1] for writing

question: Which mmap() protection flags are needed to map executable shellcode?
answers: PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE (0x1 | 0x2 = 0x3)
PROT_EXEC | PROT_READ (0x4 | 0x1 = 0x5)
PROT_EXEC | PROT_WRITE (0x4 | 0x2 = 0x6)
PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE | PROT_EXEC (0x1 | 0x2 | 0x4 = 0x7)
correct: PROT_EXEC | PROT_READ (0x4 | 0x1 = 0x5)

question: When does a format string vulnerability occur in C?
answers: When a user-controlled string is passed directly as printf()'s format argument
When a buffer smaller than the format string is passed to sprintf()
When snprintf() is called with a NULL format string pointer value
When scanf() reads a format string longer than the destination buffer
correct: When a user-controlled string is passed directly as printf()'s format argument

question: What does the %n format specifier do when used in printf()?
answers: Prints a null-terminated string argument read from the stack
Prints the number of characters written so far to output
Writes the count of bytes printed so far into a pointed-to integer
Terminates format string processing at the current position
correct: Writes the count of bytes printed so far into a pointed-to integer

question: What is a use-after-free (UAF) vulnerability?
answers: Using a pointer to heap memory that has been returned to the allocator
Freeing the same heap chunk pointer twice in succession
Writing past the end of an allocated heap buffer into adjacent chunks
Allocating more memory than is available causing malloc() to return NULL
correct: Using a pointer to heap memory that has been returned to the allocator

question: What is a double-free vulnerability?
answers: Accessing a heap pointer after the memory chunk has been freed once
Freeing the same heap pointer twice, corrupting the allocator metadata
Writing past the allocated bounds into the next heap chunk header
Allocating the same memory chunk from two threads simultaneously
correct: Freeing the same heap pointer twice, corrupting the allocator metadata

question: What does argv[0] contain when a C program is executed normally?
answers: The first command-line argument passed after the program name
The total number of arguments including the program name
The name or path of the program itself
NULL to mark the beginning of the argument vector array
correct: The name or path of the program itself

question: Where are environment variables stored in the initial Linux process memory layout?
answers: In the .data segment of the ELF binary below the BSS section
On the initial stack above argv[], below the process name string
In a separate mmap region exclusively managed by the dynamic linker
In the kernel's PCB (process control block), inaccessible from userspace
correct: On the initial stack above argv[], below the process name string

question: What does getenv() return when the requested environment variable does not exist?
answers: An empty string ("") to indicate the variable is unset
A pointer to a null byte at a well-known fixed address
NULL
-1 cast to a (char *) pointer value
correct: NULL

question: What is the numeric value of the O_RDONLY flag passed to open()?
What is the numeric value of the O_WRONLY flag passed to open()?
What is the numeric value of the O_RDWR flag passed to open()?
What is seccomp used for on Linux?
answers: Encrypting data written to file descriptors within a sandboxed process
Restricting the set of system calls a process is permitted to invoke
Sandboxing network connections using per-process BPF firewall rules
Enforcing memory page permissions to prevent privilege escalation
correct: Restricting the set of system calls a process is permitted to invoke

question: What does ptrace() allow a process to do on Linux?
answers: Trace network packets sent and received by another process
Inspect and control the execution and memory of another process
Monitor all filesystem calls made by a child process in real time
Attach a live debugger to the running kernel image directly
correct: Inspect and control the execution and memory of another process

question: What does mprotect() with PROT_NONE do to a memory region?
answers: Makes the region read-only, blocking all write and execute access
Removes all access permissions — any access triggers a segfault
Marks the region as execute-only, preventing reads and writes
Marks the region as copy-on-write for subsequent fork() calls
correct: Removes all access permissions — any access triggers a segfault

question: What is the default maximum number of open file descriptors per process on Linux?
What does strace do on Linux?
answers: Traces shared library calls made by a process to glibc functions
Traces all system calls and signals received by a target process
Profiles CPU usage and hardware cache misses for a running process
Traces network packets associated with a target process by PID
correct: Traces all system calls and signals received by a target process

question: What does ltrace do on Linux?
answers: Traces all system calls and signals received by a target process
Traces shared library (libc) function calls made by a process
Profiles memory allocation and deallocation events in a process
Traces all kernel module load and unload events system-wide
correct: Traces shared library (libc) function calls made by a process

question: What does /proc/self/fd/ contain?
answers: The raw binary data of the currently executing process image
Symbolic links to each file currently open in the process
The raw bytes of the process's full virtual memory mapping
A plain-text list of available file descriptor numbers
correct: Symbolic links to each file currently open in the process
